Junglee_Internal_Guidlines:
Junglee gaming internal guidlines : •Compliance with Legal and Regulatory Requirements
	• Age Restrictions: No gaming advertisement shall depict any person under the age of 18 years, or who appears to be under the age of 18, engaged in playing a real money game or suggest that such persons can play these games​.

	• Responsible Gaming Messaging
	• Disclaimers: All advertisements shall carry a disclaimer stating: "This game may be habit-forming or financially risky. Play responsibly."

	• Transparency and Honesty
	• No Misleading Information: Advertisements must not misrepresent the odds of winning, the nature of the games, or the potential outcomes. Avoid using deceptive or ambiguous language.
	• Terms and Conditions: All terms and conditions related to promotions, bonuses, or offers must be clear and prominently displayed or linked in the advertisement. - User Journey to be decided
	• Income Representation: Advertisements should not present real money gaming as an income opportunity or an alternative employment option​.
	• Success Implication: The advertisement should not suggest that a person engaged in gaming activity is in any way more successful compared to others​.
	• Incorporating Skill-Based Terminology: Where possible and legally compliant, advertisements should include keywords such as "skill-based" to clarify the nature of the games being promoted. This helps to differentiate them from chance-based games and aligns with legal positioning.

	• Ethical Marketing Practices
	• No Exploitation: Do not create advertisements that exploit vulnerable individuals, including minors or those with addictions (gaming or otherwise).
	• No Pressure Tactics: Avoid aggressive marketing tactics that pressure individuals to participate beyond their means. (Incorporate Dark Pattern Guidelines)
	• Cultural Sensitivity: Ensure that advertisements are respectful and do not offend cultural, religious, politics, or social values or any aspect of gender identity and sexual orientation.

	• Brand Representation
	• Consistency: All advertising materials should align with the company’s branding guidelines, including logos, colours, and tone of voice.
	• Celebrity Endorsements: If using endorsements, ensure that the celebrity is appropriate and that any claims made are truthful and not misleading.

	• Social Media and Digital Marketing
	• Targeting: Use age-gating/ geo-fencing features on social media platforms to prevent minors or users from restricted states from viewing advertisements.
	• Influencer Marketing: Ensure influencers disclose any partnerships or promotions related to real money gaming and follow platform-specific guidelines.

	• Internal Review Process
	• Pre-Publication Legal Review: If necessary, advertisements must be reviewed by the legal team or a compliance officer before being released to ensure they meet all guidelines.
	• Ongoing Monitoring: Continuously monitor the effectiveness and compliance of advertisements, making adjustments as necessary.
	• Appropriate Agreements: Business teams must ensure that appropriate agreements are executed for advertising partnerships with affiliates and third parties before initiating such partnerships.

	• Ensuring Compliance with Indian IP Laws
	• Assumption of Ownership: Assume all content (images, music, text, videos, etc.) is protected by IP rights unless explicitly stated otherwise.

	• Licensed Content Use: Use only content that is either created in-house, licensed, or used with explicit permission. Do not use copyrighted material from third parties unless you have a proper license. While in general, the use of photographs or stills from videos requires proper licensing, there is an exception for content that is sufficiently transformative. In cases where the visual content is part of a meme or other creative work, and the use is clearly transformative (such as altering the original meaning or context), it may fall under "fair use." However, this should be evaluated on a case-by-case basis, and you should get a consultation with the legal team and an express go ahead if you have any kind of doubt about this position.
rule_trademark:
Avoid Trademark Infringement: Do not use or imitate third-party trademarks (including logos, brand names, slogans) without permission. Ensure that your use does not cause confusion with an existing brand.

	• Practical tips to avoid confusion:
		• Avoid logos or designs resembling another brand's shape, font, color, or style.
		• Don’t use brand names that sound or look similar to well-known brands
		• Steer clear of slogans that mimic the tone, message, or wording of established brands.
		• Use original and distinctive branding elements.
		• Clearly indicate any endorsements or collaborations.

	• Comparative Advertising: If making comparisons with competitors, ensure the information is accurate and does not mislead consumers. The Trademarks Act, 1999, allows comparative advertising but prohibits disparagement or unfair practices.
		• Things to keep in mind:
			• Stay away from making any false claims or derogatory remarks that unfairly criticise a competitor's product or harm their reputation.
			• Do not make misleading, biased, or incomplete comparisons that confuse consumers or imply false superiority.
			• What is Permitted?
				• Truthful, Fact-Based Comparisons: Ensure claims are accurate and verifiable.
				• Focus on Strengths: Highlight your product without degrading competitors.
				• Objective Criteria: Compare using measurable facts like price or features.

	• Passing Off: Avoid any advertising practices that could be construed as "passing off" your goods or services as those of another brand, which is illegal under Indian law. For clarity, “Passing off” is when you falsely present your goods or services as those of another brand, leading users to believe they are using/buying from an already established brand.

	• Original Content Development: Focus on creating original content to avoid potential IP issues. Any third-party content incorporated into your ads must not be used unless a proper licensing agreement(s) have been executed and are still valid.

	• Agreements with Third Parties: When working with freelancers, agencies, or influencers, ensure contracts clearly outline IP ownership or licensing terms. The work should be free from any third-party IP claims.

	• Music Licensing: Use only licensed music, jingles, or soundtracks in advertisements. Unauthorized use of music can lead to significant legal consequences under the Indian Copyright Act.

	• Visual Content: Obtain proper licenses for stock images or videos. Do not use photographs, illustrations, or video clips without verifying the licensing terms.

	• Film and Celebrity Appearances: Don’t use any clips from Indian films, songs, or celebrity likenesses without permission, as these are protected under copyright and personality rights.

	• Permission for User-Generated Content: Obtain explicit permission from users before using their content in your ads. This includes clear terms of use for any UGC collected through campaigns or contests.

	• Ambush Marketing
		• Avoid Ambush Marketing: Do not associate your brand with events (like IPL or ICC World Cup) without being an official sponsor.
		• Sports Event Ads: Don’t imply sponsorship of sporting events unless Junglee has procured rights. Strictly avoid direct false associations with big sports events. Please also note that almost all athletes have their names, likeness and official photos trademarked. Please also do not use names of actual athletes (whether present or past) in your hooks unless Junglee has signed a licensing agreement with the said athlete.
		• Do not use event logos or branding or other logos or branding which look similar to the original event logos or branding without being an official sponsor, as this could lead to ambush marketing claims.

	• Getty Images and Stock Content
		• License Getty Images: Use only licensed Getty Images or stock photos. There are different kind of usage licenses, Commercial vs Editorial or Personal. Please verify the license type prior to usage.
		• Check Restrictions: Review usage limits, if any. (e.g., editorial only, no commercial use, model releases) before using any stock image.

	• AI-Generated Content: If advertisements contain content generated by artificial intelligence (AI), it is essential to review these materials for compliance with legal, ethical, and IP guidelines. The terms and usage rights of any AI tools being utilized by the team must be reviewed and approved by the legal department prior to use. The use of AI tools in generating content should adhere to all applicable laws, including copyright and trademark laws. Ensure that AI-generated content does not infringe on existing IP rights or violate the guidelines mentioned above.

	• Keep Records: Maintain documentation of all licenses, including terms of use and duration.
rule_truthful_honest:
ASCII code , Chapter I: Truthful & Honest Representation
1.1. Advertisements must be truthful.
	• Objective: Claims should be substantiated with verifiable evidence.
	• Non-compliance: Failure to provide substantiation when required by the Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI).
1.2. Claims based on research or assessments.
	• Objective: Advertisements must mention the source and date of supporting research.
	• Non-compliance: Omitting source or date makes the claim misleading.
1.3. References to other entities.
	• Objective: Advertisers cannot refer to individuals, firms, or institutions without explicit permission.
	• Non-compliance: May result in legal disputes for misuse or disparagement.
1.4. Avoid misleading facts.
	• Objective: Advertisements should not distort or omit facts, leading to consumer misunderstanding.
	• Non-compliance: Misleading visual or textual representations.
1.5. Trust exploitation.
	• Objective: Advertisements must not exploit consumer trust or lack of expertise.
	• Non-compliance: Claims causing widespread consumer disappointment or harm.
		• Examples:
			(a) Describing products as "free" without clarifying associated costs.
			(b) Claims offering products for "free" without fair pricing evidence.
			(c) Exaggerated terms like "up to five years guarantee" without clear applicability.
			(d) Exploiting consumer vulnerabilities (e.g., health conditions) with overstated benefits.
			(e) Misleading investment opportunities with vague contingencies.
			(f) Ambiguous competition rules or unfair prize distribution.
1.6. Exaggerations and humor.
	• Objective: Humorous exaggerations must be clearly identifiable as such.
	• Non-compliance: Misinterpreted claims causing consumer confusion.
1.7. Manufacturing lapses.
	• Objective: Occasional lapses in product quality may not invalidate claims if reasonable corrective actions are taken.
	• Non-compliance: Persistent failures or lack of corrective measures.
rule_non_offensive:
ASCII code: Chapter II: Non-Offensive to Public
Objective:
	To ensure advertisements align with generally accepted standards of public decency.
Key Requirement:
	Advertisements must avoid indecent, vulgar, or repulsive content, especially in the depiction of women.
Content should not cause grave or widespread offense in light of prevailing standards of decency and propriety.
Non-Compliance Implications:
	Using indecent or vulgar visuals or language, particularly in sensitive contexts, such as the portrayal of women.
rule_against_harmful:
ASCII code: Chapter III: Against Harmful Products / Services / Situations
Objective:
	To prevent the misuse of advertisements that promote harmful products, services, or situations that are hazardous to individuals or society, particularly children.

Key Points:
3.1. No advertisement shall be permitted which:
	(a) Incites crime, disorder, violence, or intolerance.
		• Non-compliance: Content encouraging illegal activities or social unrest.
	(b) Discriminates against individuals/groups based on race, caste, religion, gender, age, or other attributes.
		• Non-compliance: Derogatory remarks or visuals targeting specific groups.
	(c) Glorifies criminal behavior or conveys methods of committing crimes.
		• Non-compliance: Normalizing or teaching criminal activities, especially to children.
	(d) Damages friendly relations with foreign nations.
		• Non-compliance: Content defaming or negatively portraying other countries.
3.2. Advertisements addressed to children:
	Must not result in physical, mental, or moral harm, or exploit their vulnerability.
		• Examples of Non-compliance:
			(a) Encouraging children to enter unsafe places or interact with strangers.
			(b) Featuring dangerous acts likely to be imitated by children.
			(c) Showing children using harmful tools or substances.
			(d) Promoting tobacco or alcohol-related products.
			(e) Misusing celebrities to endorse hazardous products.
3.3. Dangerous practices:
	Ads must not show or promote dangerous actions or neglect safety.
		• Non-compliance: Depicting unsafe practices without warnings.
3.4. Legal breaches:
	Ads must adhere to laws and not omit legally required information.
		• Non-compliance: Omitting warnings or disclaimers mandated by law.
3.5. Propagation of banned products:
	Ads must not promote products/services prohibited by law.
		• Non-compliance: Indirectly promoting banned items.
3.6. Circumventing advertising restrictions:
	Ads for restricted products/services must not use proxies to bypass restrictions.
		• Non-compliance Factors:
			(a) Promoting a related unrestricted product disproportionately to target restricted ones.
			(b) Including indirect hints linking to restricted products.
			(c) Using brand names that imply association with restricted items.
rule_fair_competition:
ASCII code : Chapter IV: Fair in Competition
Objective:
	To ensure advertisements promote fair competition, provide accurate information to consumers, and adhere to accepted norms of business conduct.

4.1. Comparative Advertisements:
	Conditions for compliance:
		(a) Clearly specify what aspects of the advertiser’s product are being compared to the competitor’s product.
		(b) Ensure the comparison does not create an artificial advantage or misleading perception of a better deal.
		(c) Use factual, accurate, and verifiable comparisons.
		(d) Avoid misleading consumers about the products being compared.
		(e) Do not unfairly discredit or denigrate competitors or their products.
		• Non-compliance: Misleading comparisons, baseless claims, or defaming competitors.
4.2. Use of Names or Symbols:
	Rule: Do not exploit another firm’s goodwill, name, or trademark to gain an unfair advantage.
		• Non-compliance: Unauthorized use of competitors' names or trademarks for misleading benefits.
4.3. Plagiarism in Advertisements:
	Rule: Avoid copying the layout, slogans, music, or other creative elements from prior ads to suggest plagiarism.
		• Non-compliance: Replicating earlier ads to deceive or confuse consumers.
4.4. Complaints of Plagiarism Abroad:
	Scope: Complaints are limited to:
		(a) Ads circulated within 12 months of the initial campaign abroad.
		(b) Claims substantiated with evidence of prior use abroad.
		• Non-compliance: Filing complaints outside the specified scope or time limit.
rule_asci_guidelines:
Summary of ASCI Guidelines for Advertising of Online Gaming for Real Money Winnings
Objective Non-Compliance Rules:
	Depiction of Minors:
		Advertisements must not show individuals under the age of 18 (or those appearing to be under 18) engaging in or being associated with online gaming for real money winnings.
	Mandatory Disclaimer:

	Print/Static Ads:
		• Must include the disclaimer: "This game may be habit-forming or financially risky. Play responsibly."
		• The disclaimer must occupy at least 20% of the advertisement space.
		•
	Audio/Video Ads:
		• The disclaimer must be: "This game may be habit-forming or financially risky. Play responsibly."
	• Shown in both audio and visual formats.
	Income Opportunity Representation:
		Advertisements must not portray online gaming for real money winnings as a viable income source or an alternative employment option.
	False Perception of Success:
		Advertisements must not imply that participating in gaming activities makes someone more successful compared to others.
rule_influencer_advertising:
influencer_advertising: Guidelines for Influencer Advertising in Digital Media

Definitions

	Influencer:
		An individual with the power to influence audience purchasing decisions or opinions due to their authority, knowledge, position, or relationship with their audience.

	Non-compliance Check:
		Ensure influencers clearly disclose any advertisements or promotions.
		Verify if their authority is being used to mislead consumers.

	Virtual Influencer:
		Computer-generated entities that mimic human characteristics and behavior.
		Non-compliance Check:
			Confirm clear disclosures that the influencer is virtual and not a real person.

	Material Connection:
		Any relationship between an advertiser and influencer that may affect the credibility of the representation, including monetary compensation, free products, or other incentives.
		Non-compliance Check:
			Validate whether material connections are disclosed clearly and prominently.

	Digital Media:
		Refers to communication platforms such as social media, websites, blogs, apps, video on demand, and others that transmit content online.
		Non-compliance Check:
			Ensure all advertisements on digital media adhere to disclosure guidelines.

Preamble

	With increasing digital advertising, it is critical to differentiate content from advertisements to avoid misleading consumers. Consumers must clearly understand the commercial intent behind promotions.
	Non-compliance Check:
		Confirm that advertisements are not disguised as regular content.
		Check for adherence to clauses 1.4 (misleading by omission) and 1.5 (abuse of consumer trust).

Guidelines

	1. Disclosure:
		All advertisements by influencers must include a disclosure label indicating it is an advertisement.
		1.1 Criteria for Disclosure:
			Required when there is a material connection.
			Necessary even for unbiased evaluations or free products.
			Exemptions only for genuine, independent endorsements.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Verify if disclosures are missing for content with material connections.
		1.2 Placement of Disclosure:
			Must be prominent and upfront.
			Should not be hidden in bios, hashtags, or buried in links.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Ensure disclosures are visible and not obscured.
		1.3 Approved Disclosure Labels:
			Labels like "Advertisement," "Ad," "Sponsored," "Collaboration," etc.
			Must be in English or the language of the advertisement.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Verify if approved labels are used and understandable to average consumers.
		1.4 Virtual Influencers:
			Must disclose that they are not real humans.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Ensure virtual influencers explicitly state their virtual nature.
		1.5 Responsibility:
			Advertisers must ensure compliance with the ASCI Code.
			Influencers must rectify or remove non-compliant advertisements.
	2. Due Diligence:
		Influencers must ensure claims in advertisements are substantiated by the advertiser.
		Non-compliance Check:
			Validate the existence of supporting evidence for claims.
		Addendum I (Date: 15.07.2021)
			Disputed Advertisements:
				Evidence must be provided if a post is not an advertisement, including declarations or proof of product purchase.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Verify the submission of required evidence for disputed posts.
		Addendum II (Date: 17.08.2023)
			Health and Financial Influencers:
				Influencers must have necessary qualifications to provide advice in BFSI or health sectors.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Confirm influencers disclose qualifications and certifications prominently.
				Validate claims with supporting qualifications.
			Requirements:
				BFSI influencers must register with SEBI or possess relevant licenses.
				Health influencers must have appropriate medical or health-related certifications.
			Disclosure Placement:
				In videos, qualifications must be highlighted at the start.
				In blogs or text, qualifications should precede content.
				In audio, disclosures must occur at the beginning.
			Non-compliance Check:
				Validate proper placement of qualification disclosures.
rule_misleading_advertisment:
misleading_advertisment : The Guidelines for Prevention of Misleading Advertisements and Endorsements for Misleading Advertisements, 2022, issued by the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) under Section 18 of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, set forth strict regulations to prevent false or misleading advertisements. These guidelines apply to all forms of advertisements, regardless of medium, and impose obligations on manufacturers, service providers, traders, advertising agencies, and endorsers whose services are used for advertising.

1. Conditions for a Valid Advertisement
An advertisement is considered valid and non-misleading only if:

- It contains truthful and honest representation.
- It does not exaggerate the accuracy, scientific validity, or performance of a product or service.
- It does not misrepresent consumer rights as a unique feature of the advertiser's offer.
- It does not claim universal acceptance if there is a significant scientific division regarding the claims.
- It does not create fear regarding consumer security to coerce them into purchasing.
- It does not mislead consumers by citing unverified or unsubstantiated claims.
- It complies with sector-specific regulations.

2. Bait Advertisements (Deceptive Pricing Tactics)
A bait advertisement, which attracts consumers with a low price but fails to provide adequate stock, must:

- Ensure reasonable availability of goods and services as per demand.
- Clearly disclose stock limitations, particularly when demand is expected to exceed supply.
- State if the advertisement is only for market demand assessment.
- Not mislead consumers about market conditions or product availability to force sales under unfavorable terms.

3. Surrogate Advertisements (Indirect Advertising of Restricted Products)
Advertisements cannot bypass restrictions on banned products (e.g., tobacco, alcohol) by advertising under a different name or category.
Any brand name, logo, color scheme, or presentation linked to a prohibited product is considered surrogate advertising.
However, if a brand name is used for a permitted product separately and genuinely, it is not considered surrogate advertising unless misleading.

4. Free Claims Advertisements
Advertisements must not:

- Label a product "free" if the consumer incurs any hidden costs except for unavoidable expenses like delivery.
- Use false or misleading "free trial" offers that require non-refundable purchases.
- Reduce the quality or quantity of a product to compensate for a "free" offer.
- Describe any part of a package deal as "free" unless it is truly independent of the paid product.

5. Protection Against Misleading Advertisements Targeted at Children
Advertisements targeting children must not:

- Exploit their credulity, inexperience, or loyalty.
- Encourage dangerous behavior.
- Exaggerate product features to create unrealistic expectations.
- Encourage peer pressure by implying children will be ridiculed for not buying a product.
- Use words like "only" or "just" to make the price seem lower than it actually is.
- Feature sports or film personalities endorsing prohibited products (e.g., tobacco, alcohol).
- Blur the lines between fantasy and reality, making it hard for children to judge product performance.
- Promote junk food, carbonated drinks, or unhealthy snacks on children’s media platforms.
- Offer promotional gifts or rewards to persuade children to buy unnecessary products.
- Claim that consuming the product improves intelligence, health, or physical ability without scientific backing.

6. Prohibited Advertisements
Advertisements cannot promote goods or services that are illegal, restricted, or prohibited by law.
Advertisements should not use deceptive comparisons or false advantages over competitors.

7. Disclaimers in Advertisements
Disclaimers cannot contradict, hide, or weaken the main claim of the advertisement.
The disclaimer must be in the same language as the advertisement.
The font size must match the claim text.
The disclaimer must be clearly visible, prominent, and legible.
If a voice-over disclaimer is used, it must be played at the same speed and prominence as the original claim.

8. Duties of Advertisers, Service Providers, and Agencies
Claims and comparisons must be backed by independent research or assessments.
Advertisements should not disparage competitors or individuals without permission.
Advertisements must not abuse consumer trust or exploit their lack of knowledge.
Untrue or exaggerated claims meant to be humorous or attention-catching must be clearly recognizable as such.

9. Endorsements & Due Diligence Requirements
Endorsements must reflect the real opinion and experience of the endorser.
If an Indian professional is legally restricted from endorsing a product, a foreign professional from the same field is also prohibited from doing so.
Material connections between endorsers and advertisers must be fully disclosed to avoid conflicts of interest.

10. Interpretation & Authority
In case of ambiguity or dispute, the final interpretation of these guidelines rests with the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA).
